figures.gloss: The reconstructed Proto-Indo-European 'Sky Father' — the ancestor from whom Zeus, Jupiter, Dyaus and Tyr descend by regular sound-law.
figures.facet: Not a myth but a reconstruction: proof, from language alone, that the mirror was once whole.

figures.gloss: The reconstructed Proto-Indo-European dawn-goddess — after *Dyeus, the most securely reconstructed PIE deity (Mallory & Adams; M. L. West). She is the reluctant, ever-returning daughter of the sky who unbars the gates of light each morning, continued by Vedic Uṣas, Greek Ēōs, Latin Aurora, and Lithuanian Aušrinė — the last already a corpus figure (baltic).
figures.facet: A goddess recovered almost entirely from a shared root (*h2wes-, 'to shine'/'dawn') and a shared epithet-pattern rather than from any single surviving myth — proof that comparative philology alone can establish a deity's existence before her narrative can be reconstructed.
figures.provenance: M. L. West, Indo-European Poetry and Myth (2007); J. P. Mallory & D. Q. Adams, The Oxford Introduction to Proto-Indo-European and the Proto-Indo-European World (2006). Reconstructed, not attested — no PIE text survives.

figures.gloss: The reconstructed Proto-Indo-European thunder-god, his name built on *perkʷu- ('oak'; 'the striking one'), continued directly by Baltic Perkūnas and Slavic Perun (both already corpus figures) and echoed structurally by Vedic Indra, Norse Thor, and Gaulish Taranis. Norse Fjǫrgyn preserves the name only as a byname of Thor's mother, the god's own function having passed to Thor.
figures.facet: Where *Dyeus supplies the mirror's cognate sky-father anchor, *Perkʷūnos supplies its cognate storm-wielder anchor — letting several of the IE thunder-figures already in the corpus hang off a named reconstructed ancestor instead of a bare structural analogy.
figures.provenance: M. L. West, Indo-European Poetry and Myth (2007); Rick Derksen, Etymological Dictionary of the Baltic Inherited Lexicon (2015), s.v. *Perkʷūnos. Reconstructed, not attested.

figures.gloss: The reconstructed twin sons of *Dyeus, closely bound to horses, who ride to rescue the shipwrecked and storm-tossed. Continued by the Vedic Aśvins, Greek Dioskouroi (Castor and Polydeuces), and Baltic Dieva dēli — already a corpus figure.
figures.facet: One of the cleanest cases of reconstruction by convergence: three far-flung daughter traditions independently keep twin, horse-associated rescuer-gods born of the sky-father, letting *Diwós sūnū be recovered by the shared shape of the myth as much as by any shared word.
figures.provenance: Donald Ward, The Divine Twins: An Indo-European Myth in Germanic Tradition (1968); M. L. West, Indo-European Poetry and Myth (2007). Reconstructed, not attested.

figures.gloss: The reconstructed personification of the Sun, grammatically feminine in several daughter branches, crossing the sky by chariot or wheel. Continued by Baltic Saulė (already a corpus figure), Greek Helios (already a corpus figure), and Vedic Sūrya.
figures.facet: The reconstruction preserves a gender pattern later mythographers found surprising: a feminine sun is the more archaic Indo-European default, against which Greek and Vedic myth's masculine solar gods are themselves the divergence.
figures.provenance: M. L. West, Indo-European Poetry and Myth (2007); J. P. Mallory & D. Q. Adams, Encyclopedia of Indo-European Culture (1997), s.v. 'Sun.' Reconstructed, not attested.

figures.gloss: The reconstructed 'Broad One' — the Earth as mother and spouse of *Dyeus the Sky. Continued by Vedic Pṛthivī and Baltic Žemyna (already a corpus figure, sharing the *dʰǵʰem- earth-root also behind Greek khthōn and Latin humus).
figures.facet: Paired with *Dyeus, she anchors the mirror's widest single joint, World Parents: the reconstruction gives the Indo-European branch a proper name for a sky-father/earth-mother pattern found independently on nearly every continent.
figures.provenance: M. L. West, Indo-European Poetry and Myth (2007); Calvert Watkins, How to Kill a Dragon (1995). Reconstructed, not attested.

figures.gloss: The reconstructed Proto-Indo-European cosmogonic myth: Manu ('Man,' the first priest-king) sacrifices his twin Yemo ('Twin') and, with a bovine co-victim, builds the ordered world from his brother's dismembered body. Reflected in Vedic Manu and Yama, Iranian Yima, Norse Ymir (dismembered by Odin and his brothers), and — transformed — Roman Romulus and Remus.
figures.facet: Bruce Lincoln's reconstruction reads world-order as inaugurated by a killing between kin, making primordial sacrifice and first ancestor a single act: the world is raised on the body of the very first death.
figures.provenance: Bruce Lincoln, 'The Indo-European Myth of Creation,' History of Religions (1975), and Death, War, and Sacrifice (1991); J. P. Mallory & D. Q. Adams, Encyclopedia of Indo-European Culture (1997), s.v. 'Twin.' Reconstructed, not attested — the only creation narrative recoverable for this tradition.

figures.gloss: The reconstructed 'Third Man,' who — aided by the storm-god *Perkʷūnos — recovers stolen cattle from a three-headed serpent. Anchored by Calvert Watkins's recovered poetic formula *(h₁é)gʷʰent h₃égʷʰim ('he slew the serpent'), reconstructed from cognate collocations across the family. Reflected in Vedic Indra's slaying of Vṛtra (with the minor figure Trita Āptya preserving Trito's name) and, per the sources, in Avestan Thraētaona's defeat of the serpent Aži Dahāka.
figures.facet: The formula's survival as an actual reconstructible phrase, not merely a shared plot outline, is the strongest single piece of evidence that PIE speakers already told this story in fixed poetic language, not just a fixed narrative shape.
figures.provenance: Calvert Watkins, How to Kill a Dragon: Aspects of Indo-European Poetics (1995); M. L. West, Indo-European Poetry and Myth (2007). Reconstructed, not attested.

figures.gloss: A proposed royal rite binding king and horse, compared across the Vedic aśvamedha (a stallion released to wander a year, then sacrificed as the king symbolically unites with a mare), the Roman October Equus (a war-horse sacrificed to Mars each October), and the Irish kingship rite Gerald of Wales describes in the Topographia Hibernica (c. 1188), in which a new king ritually mates with a white mare that is then slaughtered, cooked, and its broth used to bathe and feed him.
figures.facet: Jaan Puhvel's comparison is the field's own test case for how far cross-cultural ritual comparison can be pushed before it strains: the three rites share startlingly specific details, but are separated by vast distances in time and space, and the comparison remains actively debated rather than settled — offered here at a lower confidence than the deities above, as the sources themselves flag.
figures.provenance: Jaan Puhvel, 'Aspects of Equine Functionalism,' in Analecta Indoeuropaea (1981), and Comparative Mythology (1987); Gerald of Wales, Topographia Hibernica (c. 1188). Reconstructed by comparison, not attested as a single rite, and debated.

edges.note: Zeus < *Dyēus by regular sound-law — the same word, three thousand years apart.
Iu-piter < *Dyēus-ph₂tēr — 'Sky-father' preserved almost intact in the compound.
Dyáuṣ-pitṛ́ — the Vedic form that first proved the whole family is one.
Týr < Tīwaz < *Dyēus — the sky-father's name survived even as his rank did not.

Both are sky-dwelling 'father' makers who recede above the lived world — but Dyeus is a reconstructed Indo-European god by descent, while Baiame's kinship is purely structural, an independent and far older sky-All-Father. The shared shape is the datum, never a cognate.
Two sky-fathers across unrelated families — but where Dyeus stays the active head of his pantheon, Nyame WITHDRAWS. The convergence on sky-sovereignty makes the divergence (deus otiosus) legible.
Lithuanian Dievas / Latvian Dievs continues *Dyeus directly — among the least-altered reflexes of the reconstructed Sky-Father's name.
A sky-word that may once have named a sky-god — but Basque is a NON-IE isolate, so any echo of Dyeus is areal/typological resemblance, NOT descent; a deliberately low tier.
GOLD SEAM: The ancient linguistic and structural archetype of the bright, day-lit sky as the seat of supreme patriarchal authority.
A loose parallel only — Mahatala is a bright sky-lord, but he is one half of a dyad rather than the autonomous PIE sky-father; the resemblance is the sky-association, not the structure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct linguistic and functional descent from the Proto-Indo-European sky father.
The deepest divergence in the mirror: against the sky-father at the head of nearly every pantheon, the Loka enthrones no one — an uncreated, fatherless cosmos that needs no god to hold it up.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial, luminous sky-father archetype.
The sky-father shape met far outside the Indo-European family; proof the joint is convergent, an heirloom of no single tongue. Never a cognate across families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct linguistic and functional descent from the Proto-Indo-European daylight sky father (*dyēws).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the active, anthropomorphic, storm-wielding daytime sky father distinct from the abstract firmament.
GOLD SEAM: Direct, reconstructed linguistic and functional descent from the Proto-Indo-European sky-father.
A remote sky-elder at the origin, but inverted into a miser — a withdrawn-creator read against the Indo-European luminous sky-father. Speculative, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme, radiant sky father who observes the world from on high but rarely intervenes directly in the mundane affairs of humans.

A supreme sky-dwelling lord at the head of the cosmos — Yúcahu invisible above as the reconstructed Indo-European *Dyēus is the bright sky-father — but the Taíno god is a mother-born provider of cassava, so the parallel is faint and flagged as a reach.
The sky-father shape reached down a wholly separate (Altaic, non-IE) road — the brightest proof that this joint is convergent, NOT an Indo-European inheritance. Never a cognate.
Both stand at the head of their world's sequence, but Dyeus is an active sky-sovereign while Unkulunkulu is the first ancestor merged with the creator — sky-firstness versus lineage-firstness.
Aušrinė continues *H₂éwsōs directly by the *h2ews- root — the reconstructed ancestor node the existing Baltic shard's own flagged Ausrine–Helios edge was missing.
The reconstructed dawn-goddess as daughter of the reconstructed sky-father — the *Dyeus-family relationship the Baltic and Vedic reflexes both preserve in outline.
Baltic Perkūnas continues *Perkʷūnos directly and almost unaltered — among the least-changed reflexes of the reconstructed thunder-god's name.
Slavic Perun continues *Perkʷūnos directly, the closest surviving cognate pair in the whole IE storm-cluster along with Perkūnas.
The thunder-vs-serpent template shared structurally with Vedic Indra, though Indra's name is not a cognate of *Perkʷūnos — the same caveat the existing Perkūnas–Indra edge already carries.
Two distinct reconstructed sky-associated gods — sovereignty versus storm-violence — later fused in some daughter branches (Zeus, Indra) but kept separate in Baltic Dievas/Perkūnas.
Baltic Dieva dēli ('Sons of God') continue *Diwós sūnū by name and by myth — courting and rescuing the Sun-Maiden, the reconstructed twins' defining act.
The Divine Twins are, by their reconstructed name, literally the Sons of *Dyeus.
Baltic Saulė continues *Seh₂ul directly, keeping both the root and the feminine gender of the reconstructed sun.
Greek Helios continues the *seh2ul-/*sh2wen- sun-word by regular sound-law, though Greek shifted the deity's grammatical gender to masculine.
Vedic Sūrya continues the same reconstructed sun-word, masculine as in Greek, driving the solar chariot across the sky.
Baltic Žemyna continues the *dʰǵʰem- earth-root behind the reconstructed Earth-Mother, kissed at dawn and poured the first drink of every meal.
The reconstructed World Parents: Earth as spouse of the reconstructed Sky-Father, the joint from which the daughter traditions' own sky/earth couples descend.

Earth personified as mother and source; Gaia and the reconstructed Earth-Mother share the function and the wider *dʰǵʰem- semantic field (via chthōn), but Gaia's own name is not a proven cognate — a parallel, not a shared word.
Vedic Yama continues *Yemo by name, and Vedic Manu preserves *Manu directly — the pairing that first let Lincoln reconstruct the whole cosmogony.
Avestan/Zoroastrian Yima continues *Yemo by the same etymon as Vedic Yama, though Iranian myth transforms the sacrificed twin into a golden-age king rather than a slain co-founder.

Vedic Indra's slaying of Vṛtra preserves Watkins's reconstructed formula '(he) slew the serpent' almost verbatim, and the minor Vedic figure Trita Āptya keeps Trito's own name alongside Indra's — an attested textual link stronger than plain structural resemblance, short of a full name-cognate.

dossier.essence: Not a practiced religion but the scholarly reconstruction of the religion of the Proto-Indo-Europeans — the prehistoric, unattested people (most likely of the Pontic-Caspian steppe, c. 4500–2500 BCE) whose language is the common ancestor of the Indo-European family. Because nothing was written, this 'tradition' exists only as an inference: features shared across the daughter traditions (Vedic, Iranian, Greek, Roman, Celtic, Germanic, Baltic, Slavic, Anatolian) are back-projected by the comparative method onto their lost source.
dossier.cosmos: Reconstructed elements include a luminous 'Sky Father,' *Dyēus ph₂tēr (ancestor of Zeus, Jupiter, Vedic Dyaus, and Norse Tyr), an Earth Mother (*Dʰéǵʰōm), a Dawn goddess (*H₂éusōs), Divine Twins, and a thunder-hero. A widely reconstructed cosmogony has a primal pair — 'Man' (*Manu) and his twin (*Yemo) — from whose sacrifice the ordered world and human society are made, and a recurring myth in which a hero slays a serpent. The dead were thought to reach a pastoral otherworld across water, sometimes guarded by dogs.
dossier.practice: Also reconstructed from comparanda: an oral poetics of praise built on inherited formulae (famously 'imperishable fame,' *ḱléwos n̥dʰgʷʰitom); animal sacrifice and libation to the gods; and, arguably, a tripartite social-religious ideology of priest, warrior, and producer (Dumézil's 'three functions'). Horse sacrifice is inferred from convergent rites in India, Rome, and Ireland. No institutions are attested, only inferred.
dossier.canon: None — there is no Proto-Indo-European text. The 'sources' are the reconstructions themselves, assembled from the oldest daughter literatures (the Rigveda, Homer, the Avesta, and early Roman, Norse, and Celtic material) by linguistic and comparative analysis.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds comparison itself — the demonstration, from language alone, that scattered religions descend from one lost ancestor, recovering a prehistoric faith that left no monument but its echoes in its heirs.
sources: Bruce Lincoln, 'Death, War, and Sacrifice: Studies in Ideology and Practice' (1991)
J.P. Mallory & D.Q. Adams, 'The Oxford Introduction to Proto-Indo-European and the Proto-Indo-European World' (2006)
